county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Brazil. Created 1825 from Owen, Vigo, and Putnam counties. Major coal mining county — coal mine employment records document immigrant workers including Italian, Eastern European, and African American miners. Brazil was a brick and clay products center. Clay County Historical Society. Labor movement records — Indiana coal miners were active in the United Mine Workers (UMW). Check Vigo County for pre-1825 records. Strong Catholic and Methodist church records in mining communities.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1825. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage applications, licenses, and returns from 1825. Indiana County Clerk issues marriage licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (improving detail over time).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1825. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, order books, and judgments from 1825. Indiana Circuit Court is the general trial court. Early records include debt cases, property disputes, bastardy cases, and naturalization petitions (pre-1906) that identify family relationships. Order books often record road petitions naming landowners.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Clay County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. Patents record first purchaser from the government. Searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov. Cross-reference with county deed records for subsequent transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal records — well-preserved and digitized at BLM-GLO

source_profiles.source_name: Coal Mining Employment Records — Clay County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Archives / Indiana Coal Council / Local historical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana coal mine employment records, company town records, and United Mine Workers (UMW) union records. Coal mining attracted Italian, Eastern European, and African American workers. Mine inspection reports at Indiana State Archives list workers. Company store ledgers document families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Mine records scattered; Indiana Bureau of Mines reports at Indiana State Archives

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Frankfort. Created 1830 from Tippecanoe County. Frankfort is known for the Chrysler transmission plant — 20th-century employment records. Clinton County Historical Society. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Early settlers from Ohio and Kentucky. Mulberry and Rossville area records. Strong Methodist and Christian Church (Disciples of Christ) records. County Clerk holds marriage records from 1830. Check Tippecanoe County for pre-1830 records. Prairie Township land records important.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1830. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage applications, licenses, and returns from 1830. Indiana County Clerk issues marriage licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (improving detail over time).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1830. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Clinton County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. Patents record first purchaser from the government. Searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov. Cross-reference with county deed records for subsequent transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal records — well-preserved and digitized at BLM-GLO

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in English. Created 1818 from Orange, Harrison, and Perry counties. Crawford County's rugged terrain includes Marengo Cave and the Blue River area — isolated communities mean church records are essential. One of Indiana's least populous counties historically. Leavenworth was the original county seat (1818-1893). Crawford County Historical and Genealogical Society. Check parent counties for pre-1818 records. Subsistence farming area — tax records and probate records reveal family connections. Strong Baptist and Methodist church records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1818. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage applications, licenses, and returns from 1818. Indiana County Clerk issues marriage licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (improving detail over time).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1818. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, order books, and judgments from 1818. Indiana Circuit Court is the general trial court. Early records include debt cases, property disputes, bastardy cases, and naturalization petitions (pre-1906) that identify family relationships. Order books often record road petitions naming landowners.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Crawford County Auditor, English / Indiana State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax duplicates list property owners, land descriptions, and property values from 1818. Indiana county auditor records. Early tax lists serve as census substitutes. Indiana State Archives holds some microfilmed county tax records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some tax duplicates at Indiana State Archives; others remain at county level

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Crawford County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. Patents record first purchaser from the government. Searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov. Cross-reference with county deed records for subsequent transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal records — well-preserved and digitized at BLM-GLO

source_profiles.source_name: Ohio River Commerce Records — Crawford County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Archives / Local historical societies / Kentucky river communities
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Steamboat records, ferry licenses, river pilot records, and flatboat commerce. Ohio River counties had close economic ties to Kentucky — cross-reference Kentucky county records across the river. Early Indiana settlement came primarily via the Ohio River.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Ferry licenses at county level; steamboat records at NARA and historical societies

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Washington. Created 1816 from Knox County — one of the original counties at statehood. Daviess County Historical Society in Washington, IN. Strong Amish community in the Montgomery-Odon area — Amish church records and The Budget newspaper. German Catholic parish records in Cannelburg and surrounding areas. BLM-GLO federal land patents. White River valley settlements. Check Knox County for pre-1816 records. Washington was an important early Indiana town on the national road to Vincennes.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1816. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage applications, licenses, and returns from 1816. Indiana County Clerk issues marriage licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (improving detail over time).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1816. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, order books, and judgments from 1816. Indiana Circuit Court is the general trial court. Early records include debt cases, property disputes, bastardy cases, and naturalization petitions (pre-1906) that identify family relationships. Order books often record road petitions naming landowners.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax duplicates list property owners, land descriptions, and property values from 1816. Indiana county auditor records. Early tax lists serve as census substitutes. Indiana State Archives holds some microfilmed county tax records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some tax duplicates at Indiana State Archives; others remain at county level

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Daviess County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. Patents record first purchaser from the government. Searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov. Cross-reference with county deed records for subsequent transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal records — well-preserved and digitized at BLM-GLO

source_profiles.source_name: Amish and Mennonite Church Records — Daviess County
source_profiles.repository: Mennonite Historical Library, Goshen College / Heritage Historical Library, Aylmer ON / Local church records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Amish church district records, family group sheets, Budget and Die Botschaft newspaper correspondence, and Mennonite church records. Records may be in German/Pennsylvania Dutch. Mennonite Historical Library at Goshen College is a premier repository. Family histories compiled by Amish genealogists are often in private collections.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Many Amish records in private family collections; Mennonite Historical Library has compiled indexes and microfilm

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Auburn. Created 1835 from Allen and unorganized LaGrange territory. DeKalb County Historical Society in Auburn. Auburn Automobile Company and Auburn Cord Duesenberg Museum document the auto heritage area. Cedar Creek and St. Joseph River valley settlements. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Garrett was a major railroad junction — Baltimore & Ohio Railroad employment records. Strong German and Scots-Irish heritage. Check Allen County for pre-1835 records. Waterloo and Butler area records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1835. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1835. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for DeKalb County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. Patents record first purchaser from the government. Searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov. Cross-reference with county deed records for subsequent transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal records — well-preserved and digitized at BLM-GLO

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Lawrenceburg. One of Indiana's earliest counties (1803), created from Clark. Lawrenceburg is on the Ohio River — steamboat and ferry records important. Dearborn County Historical Society. Whiskey production heritage — early distillery and tavern license records. Aurora and Rising Sun area (before Ohio County split off). Strong German and Irish immigrant communities. Cincinnati is directly across the river — cross-reference Hamilton County, OH records. Whitewater Canal records at Indiana State Archives.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1803. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.

Early records include debt cases, property disputes, bastardy cases, and naturalization petitions (pre-1906) that identify family relationships. Order books often record road petitions naming landowners.

source_profiles.repository: Dearborn County Auditor, Lawrenceburg / Indiana State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax duplicates list property owners, land descriptions, and property values from 1803. Indiana county auditor records. Early tax lists serve as census substitutes. Indiana State Archives holds some microfilmed county tax records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some tax duplicates at Indiana State Archives; others remain at county level

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Dearborn County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. Patents record first purchaser from the government. Searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov. Cross-reference with county deed records for subsequent transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal records — well-preserved and digitized at BLM-GLO

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wabash and Erie Canal (longest canal ever built in North America, 468 miles) and Whitewater Canal construction records, land purchases, worker rolls, and canal-related property records. Canal workers included many Irish immigrants. Canal land grants at Indiana State Archives.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Canal Commission records at Indiana State Archives; some published by Indiana Historical Bureau

source_profiles.source_name: Underground Railroad and African American Records — Dearborn County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Archives / Indiana Historical Society / Levi Coffin House
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana was a free state but had Black Codes restricting African American settlement. Court records include freedom suits, fugitive slave cases, and Black Code enforcement. Levi Coffin of Fountain City (Wayne County) was the 'President of the Underground Railroad.' Quaker meeting records document abolitionist activity.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Scattered across local historical societies and Indiana State Archives; some published accounts

source_profiles.source_name: Ohio River Commerce Records — Dearborn County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Archives / Local historical societies / Kentucky river communities
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Steamboat records, ferry licenses, river pilot records, and flatboat commerce. Ohio River counties had close economic ties to Kentucky — cross-reference Kentucky county records across the river. Early Indiana settlement came primarily via the Ohio River.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Ferry licenses at county level; steamboat records at NARA and historical societies

source_profiles.repository: Dearborn County Clerk, Lawrenceburg / NARA Chicago
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificates. Pre-1906 records at county Circuit Court (County Clerk). Post-1906 copies at NARA Chicago. Indiana had significant German, Irish, Swiss, and later Eastern European and Mexican immigration.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Many Indiana naturalization records indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry; some at Indiana State Archives

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana provided approximately 197,000 soldiers to the Union Army. Adjutant General records, enlistment papers, and discharge records at Indiana State Archives. Indiana Soldiers and Sailors Monument (Indianapolis). W.H.H. Terrell's 'Report of the Adjutant General of the State of Indiana' (8 vols.) published rosters.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Indiana Adjutant General records well-preserved at Indiana State Archives; indexed online

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Greensburg. Created 1821 from unorganized territory. Greensburg's famous courthouse tower tree attracts visitors, but genealogists should focus on Decatur County Historical Society and well-preserved county records from 1822. Strong German Lutheran heritage in Westport and Clarksburg areas. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Flatrock River and Salt Creek valley settlers. New Point and Sandusky area records. Strong Methodist and Lutheran church records. County Clerk holds marriage records from 1822.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1821. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Muncie. Created 1827 from Randolph County. Muncie is the setting for the famous 'Middletown' sociological studies (Robert and Helen Lynd, 1929, 1937) — Ball State University Archives hold related materials. Ball Brothers glass manufacturing (Ball Corporation) records document workers. Delaware County Historical Alliance. White River valley settlements. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Strong Quaker influence in early settlement. Indiana gas boom (1880s-1890s) brought industrial growth. Check Randolph County for pre-1827 records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1827. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.

source_profiles.source_name: Automobile Industry Employment Records — Delaware County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Library / Indiana Historical Society / Local historical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana was a major early automobile manufacturing center. Companies include Studebaker (South Bend), Haynes-Apperson (Kokomo), Duesenberg (Indianapolis), and later GM, Chrysler plants. Employment records, company newsletters, and union records document worker families across central Indiana.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Scattered across local repositories; Indiana State Library and Indiana Historical Society hold some collections

source_profiles.repository: Delaware County Clerk, Muncie / NARA Chicago
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificates. Pre-1906 records at county Circuit Court (County Clerk). Post-1906 copies at NARA Chicago. Indiana had significant German, Irish, Swiss, and later Eastern European and Mexican immigration.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Many Indiana naturalization records indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry; some at Indiana State Archives

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana provided approximately 197,000 soldiers to the Union Army. Adjutant General records, enlistment papers, and discharge records at Indiana State Archives. Indiana Soldiers and Sailors Monument (Indianapolis). W.H.H. Terrell's 'Report of the Adjutant General of the State of Indiana' (8 vols.) published rosters.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Indiana Adjutant General records well-preserved at Indiana State Archives; indexed online

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Jasper. Created 1817 from Pike County. Very strong German Catholic heritage — Dubois County is one of Indiana's most heavily German-descended counties. St. Joseph parish (Jasper) records from 1838 are critical. Dubois County Historical Society and Museum. Ferdinand and Celestine area church records. Patoka River valley. BLM-GLO federal land patents. St. Meinrad Archabbey (1854) holds extensive church records and local historical materials. German-language newspapers published in Jasper. Huntingburg and Holland area records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1817. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.source_name: German Catholic Parish Records — Dubois County
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Indianapolis / Diocese of Evansville / Parish offices
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptism, marriage, and burial records from German Catholic parishes. Many parishes kept records in Latin or German. Parish registers often include parents' birthplaces in Germany. St. Meinrad Archabbey (Dubois/Spencer) holds significant church records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish records generally well-preserved; some microfilmed by FamilySearch

source_profiles.repository: Dubois County Clerk, Jasper / NARA Chicago
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificates. Pre-1906 records at county Circuit Court (County Clerk). Post-1906 copies at NARA Chicago. Indiana had significant German, Irish, Swiss, and later Eastern European and Mexican immigration.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Many Indiana naturalization records indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry; some at Indiana State Archives

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Goshen. Created 1830 from Allen and unorganized Cass territory. Major Amish and Mennonite settlement area — Elkhart County has one of the largest Amish populations in the US. Mennonite Historical Library at Goshen College holds outstanding genealogical records. Elkhart County Historical Museum. RV (recreational vehicle) industry capital — Elkhart and Goshen factory records. St. Joseph River valley. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Die Botschaft and The Budget newspapers document Amish community events. Nappanee and Middlebury Amish records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1830. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.repository: Mennonite Historical Library, Goshen College / Heritage Historical Library, Aylmer ON / Local church records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Amish church district records, family group sheets, Budget and Die Botschaft newspaper correspondence, and Mennonite church records. Records may be in German/Pennsylvania Dutch. Mennonite Historical Library at Goshen College is a premier repository. Family histories compiled by Amish genealogists are often in private collections.

source_profiles.repository: NARA, Washington DC / Indiana State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Potawatomi Nation records including treaties, Trail of Death removal records (1838), land allotments, and agent correspondence. 859 Potawatomi were forcibly removed from Indiana to Kansas in September 1838. Chief Menominee's band. Pokagon Band of Potawatomi retained some lands.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records at NARA Record Group 75; Trail of Death records well-documented
